query: evidence regarding UK radiologists’ training and support in suspected child abuse

retrieved_docs.title: Child Physical Abuse and Neglect -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory screening for abdominal injury (assessment of liver and pancreas function and urinalysis) is recommended in children under age 5 because the physical exam has a low sensitivity for the detection of abusive intrabdominal injuries in young children. An abdominal CT scan should be performed if screening laboratory tests are elevated. A skeletal survey and repeat survey in 3 weeks are indicated in children younger than 2 years to identify occult fractures and assess bone health. Occult fractures are found on an initial skeletal survey 11% of the time, in infants with bruising 50% of the time, on a follow-up skeletal survey 46% of the time, and in siblings of abused children 12% of the time. The neurologic exam lacks sensitivity for AHT. Thus, a head CT in potentially abused infants younger than 6 months is recommended. If intracranial injuries are noted, retinal examination by a pediatric ophthalmologist and MRI/MRV of the brain and spine are recommended. Occult drug exposure has increasingly been noted in victims of physical abuse, and some centers recommend comprehensive urine drug testing for children younger than age 5. It is important to remember that the lack of additional injuries on occult injury surveillance does not lessen the concern for abuse of the initial injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Pennsylvania Child Abuse Recognition and Reporting -- Pearls and Other Issues -- What information should I include in a report of suspected child abuse?
retrieved_docs.content: The actions taken by the person making the report, including those actions taken under sections 6314 (relating to photographs, medical tests, and x-rays of a child subject to report), 6315 (relating to taking a child into protective custody), 6316 (relating to admission to private and public hospitals) or 6317 (relating to mandatory reporting and postmortem investigation of deaths)

retrieved_docs.title: Child Abuse and Neglect -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation of suspected child abuse involves a combination of imaging and laboratory studies to detect occult injuries, rule out medical conditions that mimic abuse, and guide clinical decision-making. Skeletal surveys are crucial in children under 24 months, as they can reveal additional injuries in up to 26% of cases. Chest and abdominal CT scans help identify rib fractures and internal injuries, while cranial CT or MRI is essential for diagnosing AHT. Laboratory tests assess underlying metabolic or bleeding disorders that may predispose children to fractures or bruising, ensuring a comprehensive approach to diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: Abuse and Neglect(Archived) -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the brain to rule out any intracranial hemorrhages [13] [36] Chest radiographs to rule out thoracic injuries or the presence of any acute fractures or fractures in various different stages of healing [13] [36] Global skeletal surveys in children that images subtle metaphyseal, rib, and other injuries specific for abuse [37] Computed tomography of the abdomen to rule out the presence of any intraabdominal injuries particularly duodenal or pancreatic injuries, but also liver, spleen, kidney, adrenal gland, mesentery, or intestinal injuries [13] Ophthalmologic evaluation to rule out the presence of retinal hemorrhages [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Pennsylvania Child Abuse Recognition and Reporting -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consultations may include the following: Neurosurgery Ophthalmologist Orthopedic surgeon Child protective services Social Work Psychiatrist

retrieved_docs.title: Child Abuse and Neglect -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: The presenting signs, symptoms, and diagnoses determine the need for consultations with medical and surgical specialists. Child abuse pediatricians play a central role in the evaluation, diagnosis, and management of suspected maltreatment, collaborating with healthcare teams, CPS, and law enforcement to ensure accurate assessments and protect the child’s safety. Pediatric ophthalmologists assess retinal hemorrhages in cases of AHT, while hematologists evaluate for bleeding disorders that may mimic signs of abuse. Pediatric orthopedists and radiologists contribute by distinguishing inflicted from accidental trauma through physical examination and imaging. Gynecologists care for children who have experienced sexual abuse. Neurosurgeons and neurologists manage both the acute and long-term complications of AHT. In cases where abuse results in death, forensic pathologists provide essential evidence for legal proceedings.

retrieved_docs.title: Child Abuse and Neglect -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment begins with medical stabilization, followed by a thorough physical examination and appropriate diagnostic studies. Specialized care, including mental health support and forensic evaluation, is critical. Healthcare teams must collaborate with child protective services and legal systems to ensure the safety and long-term well-being of affected children. This activity explores the risk factors, epidemiology, diagnostic approaches to child abuse, and treatment and prevention, utilizing imaging and laboratory evaluations to identify occult injuries and distinguish abuse from medical conditions. This activity provides healthcare professionals with essential knowledge to recognize, diagnose, and manage cases of child abuse effectively. Additionally, this activity emphasizes the importance of interprofessional collaboration, early detection, and trauma-informed care. Enhancing healthcare professionals' awareness and response strategies enables them to contribute to early detection, provide appropriate treatment, and prevent further harm, ultimately improving outcomes for vulnerable children.

retrieved_docs.title: Child Abuse and Neglect -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify signs and symptoms of physical, emotional, and sexual abuse, as well as neglect, across different age groups. Implement trauma-informed care strategies when evaluating and treating abused children to minimize further psychological harm and support recovery. Assess the need for further diagnostic evaluation, including imaging and laboratory studies, in suspected cases. Collaborate with the interprofessional healthcare team to improve early identification, trauma-informed care, and coordinated follow-up to prevent child abuse and neglect. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Child Abuse, Maltreatment, and Neglect -- Evaluation -- Radiology Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Any child younger than 2 years for whom a concern regarding physical abuse exists should have a complete skeletal survey obtained as part of the evaluation. Additionally, any sibling younger than 2 years of an abused child should also have a skeletal survey performed. A skeletal survey consists of 21 dedicated x-rays, as recommended by the American College of Radiology. The views include the skull, spine, chest/ribs, pelvis, femur, leg, foot, humerus, forearm, and hand. If the findings are abnormal or equivocal, further dedicated x-rays of the area of concern or follow-up x-rays are indicated in 2 weeks to evaluate for healing fractures. The most common differential diagnosis of nonaccidental injury is an accidental injury. [4] [9] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Abusive Head Trauma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Recognizing, reporting, and appropriately responding to suspected abuse is a legal and ethical obligation. Although some cases of AHT may be immediately apparent, others may elude recognition, particularly when clinicians lack experience or fail to maintain suspicion. In ambiguous situations, the diagnostic process must proceed with care and completeness. The optimal management of AHT involves an interprofessional team, including nurses, primary care and emergency clinicians, radiologists, intensivists, and neurosurgeons.

retrieved_docs.title: Abuse and Neglect(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of child abuse is not simple and requires a high degree of suspicion on the part of healthcare workers who encounter the child and the family. Abused children not only present to the physician but may have encounters with nurses, pharmacists, therapists, lab technologists, and many other allied professionals and all these professionals have a legal and moral duty to report any suspicion of child abuse. Those who do not report child abuse can even incur legal penalties. When child abuse goes undetected, it carries enormous morbidity and mortality for the child. Abused children often have unhealthy development with emotional scars that remain for life. [70] [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Pediatric Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Child abuse: Non-accidental injury in babies and children is a challenging clinical conundrum. Diagnosis is crucial, and a high suspicion should always be maintained. Bone scan offers a high sensitivity exam that adds value as an adjunct to the traditional skeletal survey. [121] [122] [123] [124] [125] [126] Bone scans can also be used in additional pathologies such as sports injuries, scoliosis, trauma, back pain after surgery, osteoid osteoma, and Langerhans histiocytosis. Fluoride PET bone scans offer a high resolution and may replace the traditional bone SPECT scan when available. [127]

retrieved_docs.title: Kentucky Domestic Violence -- Evaluation -- Children
retrieved_docs.content: Consider liver and pancreas screening tests such as AST, ALT, and lipase. If the AST or ALT is greater than 80 IU/L, or lipase greater than 100 IU/L, consider an abdomen and pelvis CT with intravenous contrast. The highest-risk are those with abusive head trauma, fractures, nausea, vomiting, or an abnormal Glasgow Coma Scale score of less than 15. A troponin level should be considered if there is any evidence of chest trauma such as abrasions, bruises, rib fractures, clavicle fractures, sternal fractures, or a fractured sternum. If elevated to greater than 0.04 ng/mL, consider obtaining a CT of the chest and an echocardiogram. Imaging

retrieved_docs.title: Child Abuse and Neglect -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of suspected AHT, a cranial CT without contrast or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can detect subarachnoid and subdural hemorrhages, as well as parenchymal injuries. Cranial ultrasonography is too insensitive for diagnosing AHT. Plain radiographs may reveal skull fractures, which occur in 25% to 40% of children with AHT. However, no specific type of skull fracture is pathognomonic for AHT. [19] [28]

retrieved_docs.content: is a common extension of the basic abdominal protocol. Additional sub‑types—“Computerized tomography of chest,” “Computerized tomography of chest, abdomen and pelvis,” and “Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen”—are defined as children of the generic CT concept, reinforcing their status as specialized branches of the overall modality. Routine chest x‑ray serves as a foundational reference for many of these advanced studies, being the primary mapping source for chest‑focused and multi‑region CT examinations, including those that incorporate the brain. This structured network clarifies how basic radiographic exams (routine chest x‑ray and pelvic/abdominal CT) act as reference points for a wide array of specialized, often contrast‑enhanced, CT protocols across the chest, abdomen, pelvis and brain, facilitating clinicians’ understanding of protocol relationships, diagnostic pathways, and staging frameworks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 996
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow‑up is classified as a component of other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, indicating that it is an imaging service within the broader radiology domain. Within this domain, other diagnostic radiology and related techniques encompasses modalities such as other x‑ray and soft tissue X‑ray of the abdomen, and it also includes unlisted computed tomography procedures (both diagnostic and interventional). CT follow‑up is performed through computed tomography imaging‑action, which itself represents a method used for unlisted computed tomography procedures. Computer assisted surgery [CAS] is positioned under multiple parent categories—including other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, magnetic resonance imaging, and other CT scan—reflecting its interdisciplinary nature that combines imaging guidance with surgical intervention. CAS further includes a specific child modality, computer assisted surgery with MR/MRA, highlighting the use of magnetic resonance and magnetic resonance angiography to guide surgical procedures. Together, these relationships illustrate how CT follow‑up, various x‑ray and CT procedures, and computer‑assisted surgical techniques are interlinked within the spectrum of diagnostic radiology and advanced imaging‑guided interventions, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with a computerized tomography image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة). Various imaging procedures share this same CT image as a subordinate element: the Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen includes a computerized tomography image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة) as a child component; the Computerized tomography of chest likewise has a computerized tomography image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة) as a child; and the coronary CT angiography (تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي) also incorporates a computerized tomography image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة) as a child. This interconnection highlights that CT imaging serves as a common diagnostic output across multiple organ‑specific CT examinations and is linked to the clinical presentation captured by the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX, underscoring its relevance in patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2147
retrieved_docs.content: Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques is a broad imaging category that includes several specific subcategories. Within this group, the subcategory Other x‑ray is classified as a child of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, indicating it represents a miscellaneous set of x‑ray examinations. Additionally, Soft tissue X‑ray of abdomen is also a child of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, denoting a specific radiographic procedure focused on imaging the abdominal soft tissues. Together, these child categories illustrate the hierarchical organization of radiologic modalities under the overarching domain of other diagnostic radiology and related techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2199

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference that can be expanded into more detailed computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a CT of the chest and abdomen and a CT of the chest are directly mapped, indicating a direct and principal relationship between the plain radiograph and these focused CT studies. Additionally, the routine chest x-ray is linked as an alternative or supplementary reference for broader CT examinations, including a computerized tomography of the brain and chest as well as a computerized tomography of the brain, chest, and abdomen. These mappings illustrate how a basic chest x-ray can inform and guide the selection of more comprehensive CT imaging of multiple thoracic and cranial regions, supporting nuanced diagnostic decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 383
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest X‑ray is a central reference point for a wide range of advanced imaging procedures. Both CT of the chest, abdomen and pelvis and CT of the chest alone are designated as primary mappings from routine chest X‑ray, indicating that these computed tomography studies are often ordered as extensions of an initial plain‑film chest examination. Additional CT examinations—including computerized tomography of the brain and chest, computerized tomography of the brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, computerized tomography of the brain, chest and abdomen, and the same study with intravenous contrast medium—are linked to routine chest X‑ray as “other” mappings, reflecting their use as supplementary or follow‑up investigations when the basic chest X‑ray raises further clinical concerns.
